prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following issue. Write the full script in English, approximately 15 minutes long when read aloud at a calm pace of about 120 words per minute.

CRITICAL: This script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine. Write ONLY plain spoken prose. Absolutely NO markdown formatting — no **, ##, *, -, bullet points, numbered lists, headers, bold, or italic syntax. No section labels. Just natural flowing speech.

## Topic
Title: Anger and Aggressive Outbursts
Category: EMOTIONAL
Description: Explosive anger episodes triggered by perceived unfairness or sudden transitions.

## Person
This is for Sam (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

CRITICAL AGE REQUIREMENT: Sam is a 7-year-old child (MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD tier). Every word of this script MUST be written for a child, NOT for an adult.
- Use only simple words (1-2 syllables when possible).
- Use playful, warm, concrete language — no abstract adult concepts.
- NEVER say Sam is "normal like an adult", "behaves like an adult", or describe adult-level coping.
- NEVER use adult register, adult emotional vocabulary, or adult expectations.
- If you find yourself writing for a grown-up, stop and rewrite for a 7-year-old child.

## Therapeutic Focus
Anger and Aggressive Outbursts
Category: EMOTIONAL
Description: Explosive anger episodes triggered by perceived unfairness or sudden transitions.

## Recommendations
- Feelings Tower — name and stack feelings with colored bricks
- Worry Wall — build and knock down to practice emotional release
- Physical discharge strategies

## Sparkling Moments
- 2024-03-05: Used words to express frustration instead of hitting during a disagreement with his sister
- 2024-03-10: Built a 'feelings tower' with his dad and named each color-feeling

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "LEGO-Based Therapy for emotional regulation in children with behavioral difficulties" (2023)
   Key findings: LEGO-based therapy improved emotional regulation and reduced aggressive incidents by 45% in children aged 6-10
   Therapeutic techniques: Feelings Tower (color-coded emotional bricks); collaborative building for frustration tolerance; structured destruction and rebuild for anger release

2. "Body-based interventions for childhood anger dysregulation" (2023)
   Key findings: Physical discharge strategies (stomping, shaking) reduced escalation duration by 50% in children 6-9
   Therapeutic techniques: Anger thermometer; physical discharge; belly breathing; feelings vocabulary

## LEGO Therapeutic Play
This session should integrate LEGO building as a hands-on therapeutic activity:
- Use LEGO construction as a metaphor for the therapeutic concept (e.g., building a 'brave tower,' 'feelings wall,' or 'calm castle')
- Include at least one guided LEGO building moment with clear spoken instructions and pauses for the child to build
- Make LEGO participation optional: 'If you have some LEGO bricks, grab a few now... if not, just imagine building in your mind'
- Connect every building activity back to the therapeutic goal — the building IS the practice, not a distraction
- Name specific LEGO techniques: Feelings Tower, Worry Wall, Brave Bridge, Memory Build, or Calm Castle — whichever fits the goal

## Clinical Notes & Observations
The following notes contain clinical observations, research findings, and insights specific to this person:

1. LEGO as emotional regulation scaffold
   Sam responds well to concrete, tactile activities. LEGO building provides a physical outlet that channels energy while maintaining cognitive engagement. The act of choosing, placing, and naming bricks creates a natural slow-down in his escalation cycle. LeGoff's LEGO therapy model shows collaborative building teaches turn-taking, frustration tolerance, and joint problem-solving.

## Audio Script Requirements
- Write as spoken prose ONLY — the listener cannot see any text, they can only hear
- Use "..." (three dots) for all pauses — between sections, after instructions, within sentences. NEVER write [pause] or any bracket markers — TTS engines read them literally
- Keep sentences short: maximum 15 words each
- Use spoken transitions: "Now..." "Next..." "When you're ready..." "Good. Let's try..."
- Include breath-timing cues for breathing exercises: "Breathe in... two... three... four..."
- Vary pacing: alternate between instruction, story or metaphor, and silence
- Never give more than two instructions in a row without an ellipsis pause or encouragement
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Address Sam by name at least 3 times throughout the session
- Personalize for Sam (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- This is for a 7-year-old child — use child vocabulary, playful framing, and age-appropriate techniques throughout. Never adult-register.
- Target duration: 15 minutes (approximately 1800 words at calm pace)
- Write in English
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
script:
Hi Sam... I'm really glad you're here with me today. I heard you've been working on something really important. I heard about that tower you built with your dad... the one with all the colors for feelings. That was such a cool idea... Today, we're going to be builders again. We're going to build some tools for those big, fiery feelings that can pop up... especially when things feel unfair or when you have to stop doing something fun. Does that ever happen? It happens to lots of kids... So let's build some ways to feel better together. If you have some LEGO bricks near you, grab a handful now... Any colors or shapes you like. And if you don't have any bricks, that's totally okay. We can imagine building together. Ready? Let's start.

First, let's just sit for a moment... Feel your feet on the floor... Good. Now, pick up one of your LEGO bricks. Hold it in your hand... Feel how it's bumpy on top... and smooth on the sides. This little brick can be a feeling. What color is it? Is it a calm blue feeling? Or a sunny yellow one? You get to choose... There's no wrong answer.

Sometimes, Sam, a feeling starts small... like one single brick. Maybe it's an orange brick of frustration. Someone gets the last cookie... or it's time to turn off the game. That one orange brick feels hot in your hands. But then... another brick snaps on top. A red brick of anger. And another... and another. Before you know it, you have a whole tower of big, hot feelings. It feels wobbly and shaky... and it might want to topple over. That's what an angry outburst can feel like. It's a tower that got too tall too fast.

The good news is... we are the builders. We can learn to build our feeling towers in a new way. We can learn to see the bricks coming... and we can choose where to put them. We don't have to let the tower get so tall it falls over. We can build it strong and steady instead.

So let's practice. Put that first brick you're holding down in front of you... That's your starting place. Good.

Now, I want you to think of your body like a volcano. When you get really mad, your volcano might feel like it's about to erupt. Your face gets hot... your hands might make fists... your heart goes boom boom boom. That's your body's alarm bell. It's saying, "Whoa! Big feeling coming through!" The first thing we can do is listen to that alarm. We can feel our feet on the floor... and we can do a volcano cool-down.

Here's how. Put your hands on your tummy. Feel it go in and out as you breathe. Now, let's pretend to cool the lava. Breathe in slowly through your nose... like you're smelling a yummy flower... one... two... three... four... Good. Now breathe out through your mouth... like you're blowing out birthday candles... slowly... two... three... four... five... Let's do that two more times. Breathe in cool air... two... three... four... And blow out the hot air... two... three... four... five... One more time. In... two... three... four... And out... two... three... four... five...

Great job, Sam. You just cooled your volcano a little bit. That's a superpower.

Now, let's use our LEGO bricks. I want you to build a small wall. Just a few bricks high. Maybe four or five. This is going to be a Worry Wall. This wall can hold those "it's not fair" feelings... or those "I don't want to stop" feelings. As you snap each brick on, think of one little thing that sometimes bothers you. You don't have to say it out loud. Just think it... and snap the brick. Keep building your wall... I'll wait.

...

Good. Look at your wall. See how you built it? You put those feelings somewhere. You didn't let them stay swirling inside. You gave them a place to be. Now, sometimes, we need to let those feelings go. So here's the fun part. When you're ready... I want you to gently knock your wall over. Use just one finger. Tap it... and watch it fall. Poof! Those feelings don't have to be stuck anymore. We acknowledged them... and then we let them go. That's a way to feel strong.

Sometimes, our bodies still have lots of energy when we're mad. That's okay. We just need to let that energy out in a safe way. We can't hit or kick... but we can shake and stomp. Let's try a discharge dance. It sounds silly, and that's the point!

Stand up if you can. Now, shake your hands like they're all wet and you're trying to dry them off. Shake shake shake! Let all that buzzy energy go right out your fingertips. Good. Now stomp your feet on the ground. Stomp stomp stomp! Like a dinosaur. Get that grumpy energy out through your feet. Do that for a few more seconds...

...

Now stop. Stand still. Put your hand on your heart. Feel how it's beating? You moved that big energy right out of your body. Well done. You can sit back down now.

Let's go back to our bricks. I want you to find one special brick. This one is not for a mad feeling. This one is for a brave feeling. Think of that time you used your words with your sister instead of hitting. That was so brave. Find a brick that feels brave to you. Maybe it's a strong green brick... or a shiny gold one. Snap that brave brick onto your table. That's your Brave Brick. That brick is proof that you can do hard things.

Now, let's build a new tower. But this time, we'll build it slowly. With space between the bricks. This is a Calm Tower. Pick up any color. As you place it down, take a deep breath in... and out. Good. Now add another brick... and breathe in... and out. You are the builder. You are in charge of how fast this tower grows. See? You can feel a feeling... and breathe... and then choose the next brick. That's what control feels like.

Let's build your Calm Tower a little higher... brick by brick... breath by breath... I'll be quiet while you build.

...

Look at that, Sam. You built that. It's steady. It didn't have to fall over. You took your time. When big feelings come, you can remember this tower. You can remember to put a feeling brick down... and then take a breath... before adding the next one. You don't have to stack them all at once.

Before we finish, let's practice one more quick tool. It's called the Five Finger Countdown. Hold up one hand. Look at your thumb. Name one thing you can see right now. Say it in your head. Now your pointer finger. Name one thing you can hear... Good. Your middle finger. Name one thing you can feel... like your shirt or the chair. Your ring finger. Take one deep belly breath... in and out. Last, your pinky finger. Give yourself a little wiggle. You just used your five senses to help your brain feel calmer. You can do this anytime, anywhere.

You did amazing work today, Sam. You cooled your volcano... you built and knocked down a Worry Wall... you shook out your energy... and you built a strong, calm tower. Remember your Brave Brick. You have that inside you all the time.

Here's something you can try with your mom or dad later. Show them your Five Finger Countdown. Or build a Feelings Tower together... and tell them what each color brick means. It can help them understand what's happening inside you.

It's okay to have big feelings, Sam. All kids do. What matters is that you're learning what to do with them. And you are learning so well. I'm really proud of you. Keep being a brave builder. Whenever you need a reminder, just take a deep breath... and remember you have tools. You've got this.

If those big feelings ever feel too big, it's always okay to ask a grown-up you trust for a hug or for help. They are there for you.

Goodbye for now, Sam. Great job today.
